diagrams.title: DFD Symbols and Components
diagrams.description: This diagram illustrates the four fundamental symbols used in Data Flow Diagrams: External Entities (sources/destinations of data), Processes (data transformations), Data Stores (data repositories), and Data Flows (paths of data movement). Understanding these symbols is essential for reading and creating DFDs.
elements: Process symbol (circle/rounded rectangle) with label 'Process Order'
Data Store symbol (open-ended rectangle) with label 'Customer Database'
Data Flow arrows with labels showing data direction
diagrams.purpose: Introduces the basic notation and vocabulary of the Flow-Oriented Data Flow Model, enabling learners to recognize and interpret DFD components.

diagrams.title: Context Diagram (Level 0 DFD)
diagrams.description: This diagram shows the highest-level view of a system (Context Diagram), depicting the system as a single process surrounded by external entities. It illustrates the system boundary and shows how the system interacts with outside entities through data flows, without showing internal details.
elements: Central circle representing the entire system (e.g., 'Order Processing System')
Data flow arrows connecting entities to system with labels (Orders, Invoices, Reports)
System boundary rectangle encompassing the central process
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the system boundary concept and external entity interactions, providing a high-level overview before diving into detailed processes.

diagrams.title: Level 1 Data Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: This diagram presents a Level 1 DFD that decomposes the context diagram into major functional processes. It shows how data moves between processes, data stores, and external entities, illustrating the flow-oriented transformation of data through the system.
elements: Multiple process circles with numbered labels (Process 1, Process 2, etc.)
Data flow arrows showing data movement direction with data labels
Connecting lines showing relationships between components
Example: Order receives → Validate Order → Update Inventory → Send Confirmation
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates how the Flow-Oriented Model represents data transformation through processes, helping students understand how information flows and gets transformed within a system.
